segments.s415-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بهمن is the personal name (subject). بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (archaic/poetic bi- prefix + آمد). به + پرده‌سرای is a prepositional phrase of direction ('into/to the curtain-hall' or 'stage-chamber'); the preposition به precedes the place.
segments.s415-g42: همی بود is a poetic/periphrastic past-continuous: همی (stative/continuous marker) + بود (past 'was') = 'was standing / remained'. پیش پدر = 'in the presence of / before his father' (پیش = in the presence of; پدر = father). بر به پای is a compact, somewhat archaic locative phrase literally 'upon/at the foot' (بر = on/upon; به = to/at; پای = foot) — idiomatically 'at his father's feet'.
segments.s415-g43: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'asked' (3rd sg) placed before the subject by poetic inversion; ازو is the clitic/pronoun 'from him / of him' marking the person asked. فرخ اسفندیار is the subject (epithet + name) placed after the verb in inverted word order common in epic poetry.
segments.s415-g44: که introduces the content/indirect question clause ('that' / 'what'). پاسخ چه کرد is an interrogative content phrase with object-before-verb order typical of Persian rhetorical questions: پاسخ = 'answer', چه = 'what', کرد = past 'did/made' (here 'give' or 'make' an answer). آن یل نامدار is the subject phrase 'that renowned hero' placed after the verb (آن = that; یل = hero; نامدار = famed).
segments.s415-g45: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. بنشست is the past 'he sat down' (pile of two past verbs in sequence: heard → sat). پیش پدر = 'before his father' (پیش = in the presence of). The sequence describes quick successive actions.
segments.s415-g46: بگفت is the past 'he said / he told'. آنچ (= آن‌چ) is a relative/indefinite 'that which / what'. بشنیده is a perfect-participial form 'heard' ('what he had heard' = object). بد در بدر is a compact, somewhat elliptical phrase: بد = 'bad/ill', در + بدر locates or concerns 'Badr' (بدر here behaves like a proper name). Grammatically, بد qualifies the report and در بدر specifies its locus/subject; the exact referent (a person/place named بدر) is determined by context.
segments.s415-g47: نخستین is the adverb/adjective 'first'. درودش = درود + ـش (his greeting/salutation) is the direct object (the clitic -ش marks possession). ز is the poetic preposition 'from / to / by' used here to mark the other participant (ز رستم = 'to/for Rostam' or 'from Rostam' depending on reading of agency). بداد in this line is a past verb meaning 'gave' (he gave his greeting), but the provided sense list for بداد does not include this verbal sense, so the correct sense is not present in the supplied list.
segments.s415-g48: پس‌انگاه is an adverb 'afterwards'. گفتار او = 'his speech / his words' (گفتار = speech; او = he/his). کرد یاد is a light-verb construction where کرد ('made/did') combines with یاد ('remembrance') to mean 'he recalled / he made memory of' — i.e., 'he remembered or recounted his words'. This is a common poetic periphrasis rather than a single lexical verb.
segments.s415-g57: The phrase begins with the prepositional phrase 'به دیدار شاه' ('to/into the sight/presence of the king'), followed by the archaic past verb 'آمدستش' ('came' / 'arrived'). The subject 'نیاز' (a supplication/plea) follows the verb — a poetic verb‑before‑subject order — so the whole means 'a supplication came to the king's presence.'
segments.s415-g58: 'ندانم' is the negative present first‑person 'I do not know.' The embedded clause 'چه دارد همی با تو راز' is a question/indirect interrogative: 'چه' = 'what' is the object, 'دارد' is present 'has/possesses,' and the particle 'همی' is an archaic/emphatic/progressive marker (often marking ongoing or habitual action). 'با تو راز' = 'a secret with you.'
segments.s415-g59: The preposition 'ز' here indicates cause or source ('because of / at'), so 'ز بهمن' = 'at/from Bahman' (i.e. on Bahman's account). 'برآشفت' is an archaic past form 'became angry' and appears before the proper name 'اسفندیار' (verb‑subject inversion typical in poetry): 'Esfandiyar was angered at Bahman.'
segments.s415-g60: 'ورا' is the accusative/enclitic form 'him' (to him). 'بر سر انجمن' is a locative/prepositional phrase literally 'upon/at the head of the assembly' used idiomatically as 'before/in the presence of the assembly.' The compound 'کرد خوار' uses 'کرد' ('made/did') with the following adjective 'خوار' ('despised') as a predicative complement: 'he made him despised' (i.e. humiliated him) in front of the assembly.
segments.s415-g61: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said': the phrasing introduces reported speech). 'کز' is the contracted relative/partitive 'که از' meaning 'one of/that from,' so 'کز مردم سرفراز' = 'one of the honored/victorious people' or 'who, among the noble people...'.
segments.s415-g62: 'نزیبد' is an impersonal negative form 'it does not befit / it is not fitting.' The clause after 'که' ('that') gives the content: 'با زن نشیند به راز' = 'that he sit with a woman in private.' 'نشیند' is a present/jussive-subjunctive form (classical mood after such constructions) meaning 'sit (down)/meet,' and 'به راز' = 'in secrecy / in private.'
segments.s415-g63: 'وگر' is the conditional 'if.' 'کودکان را' uses the object marker 'را' marking 'children' as definite/affected. The verb 'بکاری' here (literally 'to plant') is used metaphorically 'to rear/bring up,' and 'بزرگ' qualifies them as grown/large/important: 'if you rear the children to be grown.'
segments.s415-g64: 'فرستی' is the second‑person present/subjunctive of 'فرستادن' ('you send' / 'you send forth') and continues the conditional idea. 'نباشد' is a negative present form meaning 'there will not be / they are not,' followed by the predicates 'دلیر و سترگ' ('brave and ...'). The line asserts that even if you rear and send them out, they will not be brave and (truly) great; note that some listed lexical senses in the data do not match these classical meanings, so a direct sense index is omitted where none fit.
segments.s415-g33: نیاید is a poetic 3rd-person negative verb meaning 'does not come/appear' (present/narrative). ز introduces the source ('from') and is followed by the noun phrase مرد خرد = 'a man of wisdom' (مرد = man + خرد = wisdom). کار بد is a noun phrase 'bad deed' where کار is the head and بد qualifies it as evil.
segments.s415-g34: ندید is simple past 'he did not see'; the independent subject او follows the verb (common in older/poetic word order). ز ما = 'from us' marks the source, and هیچ کردار بد = 'no/any evil deed' is the object phrase (هیچ here = 'no/none').
segments.s415-g35: زواره is a proper name here (a person) and بیامد is past 'came'. به + نزدیک forms a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به نزدیک = 'toward/near'). زال is the name of the person approached.
segments.s415-g36: وزان is an archaic contraction of و + ز آن ('and from that / at that'), introducing a result or reaction. روی here means 'face/sight' (i.e. 'that appearance'), and برافراخت is a past 3rd-person verb 'raised/erected' with یال 'crest/mane' as its object; رستم is the actor.
segments.s415-g37: بیامد = 'came' (past). دمان ('دامان') here means 'skirt/edge' (the edge of the land), and تا + لب + هیرمند = 'as far as the brink of the Helmand' (تا ... لب = 'up to the bank of').
segments.s415-g38: سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = his). تیز گشته is a compound: تیز 'sharp/keen' + گشته (past participle/verb 'has become') = 'has become keen/alert'. ز introduces reason 'from/because of' and بیم گزند = 'fear of harm/danger.'
segments.s415-g39: عنان را: عنان 'reins' is marked by the definite/object marker را. گران کرد is 'made heavy' (i.e. tightened the reins) with کرد as the causative/do verb. بر پیش رود is a prepositional phrase 'toward/in front of the river' (بر = 'toward', پیش = 'in front of/before', رود = 'river').
segments.s415-g40: همی بود is a continuous/past narrative 'he kept on / was continuing'. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until', and بهمن آرد درود is a subordinate clause where بهمن (a proper name) is the subject and آرد درود = 'brings/ brought greeting(s)'.
segments.s415-g9: Imperative instruction: به + محل + فعل. به ایوانها is a prepositional phrase 'into/at the iwans' (به = toward/into). تخت زرین is the direct object (takhte 'golden throne'); نهید is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person plural imperative 'place/set' (from نهاد). The word order is prepositional phrase → object → verb, common in Persian commands for emphasis.
segments.s415-g10: Two imperatives in sequence: برو here is an imperative 'go' used pragmatically like 'go and...' (introducing the next action). جامه خسرو آیین is the object phrase: جامه 'garment/robe' qualified by خسرو 'of the king/sovereign' and آیین 'ceremonial/ritual' (an adjective-like noun). نهید again is the (plural) imperative 'set/place'. The sequence means 'go and set the kingly ceremonial robe.'
segments.s415-g11: Чنان هم که introduces a comparison: چنان 'so/thus' + هم adding emphasis ('even/also'), که introduces the subordinate clause. هنگام کاوس شاه = 'at the time of Kaavus the king' (هنگام = 'at the time of'). Word order is temporal phrase after the comparative introducer.
segments.s415-g12: Comparative clause: ازان نیز ... پرمایه‌تر پایگاه. ازان here functions as 'than that' / 'from that' in a comparative construction; نیز 'also/even' adds emphasis. پرمایه‌تر is the comparative form ('more substantial/richer'), formed by پرمایه + -تر. پایگاه is the noun 'foundation/base' and completes the clause (word order puts the comparative adjective before the noun for poetic emphasis).
segments.s415-g13: بسازید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative 'make/prepare'. چیزی که باید خورش is a noun clause 'something that the feast/dishes require' or 'whatever is needed for the stew(s)': چیزی 'something', که introduces a relative clause, باید 'is necessary/ought to' and خورش 'a stew/dish' is the thing required. The verb بسازید governs the whole request.
segments.s415-g14: A specification of the required items: خورشهای خوب 'good stews/dishes' (خورشهای is plural), followed by از پی + noun to indicate sequence or consequence: از پی = 'following/one after another' and پرورش here means 'development/elaboration' or 'careful preparation' of the dishes. The segment lists what to prepare and how (good stews, produced in succession by careful preparation).
segments.s415-g15: Relative clause introduced by که linking back to prior statement: نزدیک ما 'near us' (prepositional/adverbial phrase of place), پور شاه 'the king's son' (compound noun), آمدست is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person perfect/past form 'has come' (آمد + ست). Word order places the place expression before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s415-g16: Description of his disposition: پر از کینه 'full of hatred' (پر + از = 'full of'), و 'and', رزمخواه 'war‑desiring/eager for battle' (an adjective/noun meaning one who desires war), آمدست repeats the archaic 'has come' — altogether 'he has come full of hatred and eager for battle.'
segments.s415-g49: همه دیده پیش پدر بازگفت is literally 'all (that) [he] had seen, before/unto the father, he recounted.' ديده is a past participle used nominally ('that which was seen'); پيش پدر = 'in the presence of / to his father' follows the noun phrase; بازگفت is a past tense verb 'recounted/related' placed at the end as expected in Persian SOV order, with the subject omitted (understood from context).
segments.s415-g50: همان نیز نادیده اندر نهفت: همان نیز 'that same (thing) also/indeed'; نادیده functions adverbially/'not seen' (i.e. unseen or not shown); اندر نهفت is a verb phrase where اندر = 'in/within' and نهفت (classical past of نهفتن) = 'he hid / he kept concealed.' The verb is at the end and the clause contrasts with the previous statement (he told much but kept that one thing hidden).
segments.s415-g51: بدو گفت چون رستم پیلتن = 'he said to him that / since Rostam (at) Piltan...' بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him'; گفت is the past verb 'said' (subject omitted); چون here introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'since/when'; رستم پیلتن names the person and the place (Rostam at Piltan) without an explicit verb — the verb for the subordinate clause follows in the next segment. This is typical classical/poetic clause linking.
segments.s415-g52: ندیده بود کس بهر انجمن: here ناديده + بود form the past perfect-like construction 'had not been seen / had not seen' (ندیده = past participle, بود as auxiliary). The inverted order places the participle+auxiliary before the subject (کس = 'no one' in this context). بهر انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'for/to/with respect to the gathering (assembly)'. Together the clause reads 'since no one had seen him at Piltan for the assembly.'
segments.s415-g53: دل شیر دارد تن ژنده پیل = 'he has a lion's heart, [and] a ragged-elephant body.' دل شیر is a possessive/genitive phrase 'heart of a lion' used as the object of دارد ('has'); تن ژنده پیل is a noun phrase where ژنده ('ragged') modifies پیل ('elephant') and follows the head تن ('body')—so 'a body like a ragged elephant.' دارد is a present/past habitual verb 'has' (third person).
segments.s415-g54: نهنگان برآرد ز دریای نیل: نهنگان (whales) is the direct object; برآرد is a classical 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'brings forth / lifts up' (here a hyperbolic ability); ز دریای نیل is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea of the Nile' indicating source. Word order places object before verb for emphasis (poetic), and ز is the poetic form of 'from.'
segments.s415-g55: بیامد کنون تا لب هیرمند = 'he came now up to the bank of the Helmand.' بیامد is the past tense 'came'; کنون is the temporal adverb 'now/presently'; تا here means 'as far as / up to' and introduces the place phrase لب هیرمند ('the bank/brink of the Helmand'). The verb appears early for poetic effect, and the destination phrase follows.
segments.s415-g56: ابی جوشن و خود و گرز و کمند lists the things he has: ابی جوشن = 'a blue (ابی) cuirass/hauberk (جوشن)' (adjective before noun is common in poetry); و خود = 'and himself' (خود emphasizes his own personal presence); و گرز و کمند = 'and a mace and a lasso' — a simple coordinated list introduced by و. The sequence describes his equipment/appearance on arrival.
segments.s415-g1: This is a compressed temporal subordinate clause. 'ز رستم' is a prepositional phrase modifying 'سخن' (‚the speech from Rostam‘). 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when', and 'بشنید' is the 3rd person singular past tense 'he heard'. 'بهمن' is the subject (placed before the object for poetic word order) and 'سخن' is the object 'speech'. The clause as a whole = 'When Bahman heard the speech from Rostam...'.
segments.s415-g2: 'روان گشت' is a two-word verbal expression meaning 'became in motion / set out' (گشت here = 'became/turned' used with روان). 'با' is the preposition 'with' and introduces the companion 'موبد پاک‌تن' ('the pure-bodied mobed'), where پاک‌تن is a postpositive epithet describing موبد. The verb 'گشت' is past, so the whole phrase continues the narrative started in the previous clause.
segments.s415-g3: 'تهمتن' is the subject (an epithet for the hero). 'زمانی' functions as a temporal adverb 'for a while'. The locative phrase 'به ره در' ('on/at the road' or 'at the gate/approach') precedes the verb 'بماند' (3rd person singular past: 'remained' / 'stayed'). Word order places time and place before the verb, typical in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s415-g4: This is a transitive clause: 'زواره فرامرز' is a name in the genitive/attributive order ('Farāmarz of Zowara' with the place-name first as an epithet), 'را' marks the definite direct object, and 'پیش خواند' is the compound verb 'called/summoned before (him)'. 'پیش' here is adverbial 'in the presence of / before (someone)'. The verb 'خواند' is past tense 'he summoned'.
segments.s415-g5: 'کز' functions as a subordinating/demonstrative connector 'from/that', and 'ایدر' = 'here'. The phrase 'به نزدیک دستان' is an instruction 'to near Dastān' (دستان treated here as a proper name/place), and 'شوید' is a jussive/imperative-form of شدن used for 2nd-person plural 'go/become/arrive' — an order addressed to listeners. The whole segment is an imperative direction starting at 'from here'.
segments.s415-g6: 'به نزد' is a fixed combination meaning 'to the presence of / to (someone)'. 'مه' is an archaic form here functioning as the 1st-person object (‚me / my presence‘) in older Persian syntax, and 'کابلستان' is the place-name 'Kabul region'. 'شوید' repeats the jussive/imperative 'go/come (there)'. Together: 'go to my presence in Kabulistan.'
segments.s415-g7: 'بگویید' is an imperative (2nd-person plural) 'tell/say' directing the addressee to announce what follows. It is followed by the clause 'کاسفندیار آمدست' ('Kaspandiyar has come'), where 'آمدست' is an archaic/perfect-like past 'has come'. The whole functions as a reporting command.
segments.s415-g8: This is a verbless descriptive clause typical in Persian narrative: 'جهان را' (the world) is marked by 'را' as the thing in question, 'یکی' is an indefinite pronoun 'someone / a certain one', 'خواستار' is a noun/adjective 'desirer / claimant', and 'آمدست' is the past 'has come'. Literal order puts the object before the subject/predicate: 'A claimant of the world has come.'
segments.s415-g17: This is a nominal clause: 'گوی نامدارست' uses 'گوی' in the sense of 'it is said / is said of him' (a reportive marker) followed by the predicate 'نامدارست' ('is famed'). The conjunction 'و' links a second nominal phrase 'شاهی دلیر' (lit. 'regal / kingly, brave'), where 'شاهی' is a noun/adjective meaning 'royal/kingship' and 'دلیر' is the attributive adjective 'brave'.
segments.s415-g18: 'نیندیشد' is past negative third‑person singular ('he did not think / did not imagine'). The preposition 'از' introduces the domain of thought ('concerning / about'), so 'از جنگ' = 'about the battle'. 'یک دشت شیر' is a noun phrase 'a plain (دشت) of lions (شیر)' with 'یک' as an indefinite/quantifier; poetically the genitive/attributive relation is expressed by placing شیر after دشت.
segments.s415-g19: 'شوم' is the first‑person subjunctive/optative of شدن ('may I be / let me be'), here expressing a wish or intent 'may I be before him'. 'پیش' means 'in the presence of / before', and 'او‌گر' = 'او + گر' forms the conditional 'if he…'. 'پذیرد' is third‑person present/subjunctive 'accepts' (classical Persian uses this form in conditionals) and 'نوید' = 'good news / promise' is its object.
segments.s415-g20: This is the result clause: 'به نیکی بود' literally 'there was/would be for goodness' — 'بود' here functions as a past/conditional auxiliary meaning 'would be / will be'. Word order is topical: 'هرکسی را' (literally 'to/for everyone' with the object/beneficiary marker 'را') precedes the head 'امید' ('hope'). Together: 'there would be hope of good for everyone.'
segments.s415-g21: A conditional 'اگر' clause: 'اگر نیکویی بینم' — 'if I see/notice any goodness' — where 'بینم' is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I see' (used here as 'I find'). 'نیکویی' is the thing seen ('goodness/excellence'), and 'اندر سرش' places that goodness 'in his mind/heart' ('اندر' = in, 'سر' = inner thought/mind, with possessive suffix 'ش' = his).
segments.s415-g22: 'ز' here is the poetic preposition 'of/with/from' introducing the material/contents ('of ruby and gold'). 'آورم' is first‑person present (I bring / I will bring) stating the speaker's intent; classical Persian often uses present for near future. 'افسرش' ('his helmet') is the direct object with the possessive suffix; the whole phrase means 'I will bring (for) his helmet rubies and gold' or more naturally 'I will deck his helmet with ruby and gold.'
segments.s415-g23: 'ندارم' is first‑person present negative 'I do not have' but in this idiom with 'دریغ' it means 'I do not withhold / I am not reluctant'. 'ازو' ('from him' / 'toward him') marks the beneficiary or source in classical usage. 'گنج و گوهر' ('treasure and jewels') are the things the speaker will not begrudge; 'دریغ' is a noun 'reluctance/pity' used after the verb to express 'without reluctance'.
segments.s415-g24: The coordinating negative 'نه ... و نه ...' enumerates items the speaker does not withhold. Each noun following 'نه' is an item in the list (here: برگستوان, گوپال, تیغ). The verb/negation from the previous segment ('ندارم ... دریغ') applies across the list, so the meaning is 'nor [do I withhold] X, nor Y, nor Z.'
segments.s415-g65: تو is the 2nd-person singular 'you' (subject). گردنکشان is a plural noun 'rebels/proud ones' and is marked by the object marker را (here functioning as an accusative/definite marker attached to the noun). کجا دیده‌ای is the verbal question 'where have you seen (them)?' — دیده‌ای is the 2nd-person singular perfect/present-perfect of دیدن ('you have seen'). Note the fronting of the object phrase گردنکشان را before the verb.
segments.s415-g66: که introduces a relative/content clause ('who/that') modifying گردنکشان. آواز روباه is a genitival phrase 'the cry/voice of a fox' (آواز here = 'call/cry'). بشنیده‌ای is the 2nd-person perfect 'you have heard'; in this construction the verb appears as a clause-reporting/relative predicate (classical usage often uses personal endings in ways that differ from modern colloquial patterns).
segments.s415-g67: که begins a subordinate clause ('that/for that'). رستم is the 3rd-person subject. همی is an archaic/poetic continuous/emphatic particle (here marking ongoing or emphatic action). پیل جنگی is a noun phrase 'war-elephant' (پیل 'elephant' modified by جنگی 'warlike' / 'of war'). کنی is a present-tense verbal ending used in classical poetry; although normally 3rd-person would be کند, Ferdowsi frequently uses -ی forms as an archaic 3rd-person present — functionally 'he makes/does'.
segments.s415-g68: دل is the direct object 'heart'. نامور modifies دل: 'renowned, celebrated'. انجمن is a genitive/possessive relation 'of the assembly' (the assembly's famed heart). بشکنی is another instance of the archaic/poetic present form (parallel to کنی) meaning 'he breaks/shatters' (from شکستن); word order places the object phrase before the verb, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s415-g69: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت, past 3rd-person of گفتن 'he said'). پس 'then/afterwards' follows; با پشوتن 'with/to Peshotan' marks the addressee, and به راز 'in secret' is an adverbial phrase indicating the manner (secretly/confidentially). Word order: adverbials and applicatives surround the reporting verb.
segments.s415-g70: که introduces the reported content clause. این شیر is the demonstrative noun phrase 'this lion' (a metaphorical reference, here 'lion' = brave warrior). رزم‌آور is a compound adjective 'battle-bringing/warrior' modifying شیر. جنگ ساز is a compound noun/adjective meaning 'war-maker' (ساز used agentively: 'one who makes/causes' war); the copula is omitted (zero copula common in Persian reports), so the phrase predicates that the lion is a war-maker.
segments.s415-g71: جوانی is the object 'youth'. همی is the continuous/emphatic particle (here 'keeps on' / 'continually'). سازد is the 3rd-person present 'he makes/creates' (سازد from ساختن). از خویشتن is a source phrase 'out of himself' (از = 'from', خویشتن = 'himself'). The clause means he continually fashions or renews youth from himself.
segments.s415-g72: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'; سالش is سال + enclitic possessive ش 'his' = 'his years/age'. همانا is an emphatic particle 'truly/verily'. نیامد is the past negative 'did not come'. شکن is a noun 'breaker/torturer' (here 'one who would break/overthrow him'). The clause states that, given his years, truly no breaker has come — i.e., age has produced no one capable of breaking him.
segments.s415-g25: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if'. بازگرداندم is the 1st person past (or perfect) form of بازگرداندن ('I returned/brought back') used here to form the protasis ('if I bring/bring back'). ناامید is an adjective 'hopeless' that belongs with the following clause (i.e. 'do not be hopeless'); it is left before the main negative verb for poetic suspension.
segments.s415-g26: نباشد is a negative jussive/3rd‑person form of بودن used imperatively/optatively here ('let there not be' / 'do not be') and governs مرا ('for me' / 'about me'), so ناامید نباشد مرا = 'do not be hopeless about me'. روز با او سپید is a separate predicative phrase: روز 'day' as subject, با او 'with him', سپید 'bright/white' as predicate adjective ('my day is bright with him').
segments.s415-g27: تو دانی که is the common sequence 'you know that' (تو 'you' + دانی present of دانستن 'you know' + که 'that'). آن تابداده کمند is a noun phrase: آن 'that' + تابداده a participial/adjectival form from تاب‌دادن ('twisted' / 'made by twisting') modifying کمند 'lasso/snare'.
segments.s415-g28: سر ژنده پیل is a compact poetic grouping meaning 'the (bare/stripped) head of the elephant' (سر 'head' qualified by ژنده 'ragged/stripped' and linked to پیل 'elephant'). اندر آرد به بند is a verbal phrase: اندر 'into/within', آرد (3rd‑singular of آوردن) 'brings/puts', به بند 'into fetters/bondage' (به + بند). Word order reflects poetic compression but the grammatical roles are head (object) + verb + goal.
segments.s415-g29: زواره is a proper name (subject). بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + ضمیر). گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing reported speech. مندیش (poetic/contracted form) functions as an admonition 'do not think / do not suppose' (here forming the speech's opening). ازین = از + این 'from this / henceforth' (a temporal/limiting adverbial).
segments.s415-g30: نجوید is a negative present/subjunctive of جستن used impersonally here 'does (will) not seek' (in context with مندیش, it expresses 'do not suppose that anyone will seek'). کسی 'anyone'. رزم کش is a compound where کش is the agentive suffix '‑wielder/‑fighter', so رزم‌کش 'one who takes up battle / warrior'. نیست is 'is not/there is none'. کین 'vengeance/hatred' names the motive: 'there is no warrior who fights for vengeance.'
segments.s415-g31: ندانم is the 1st‑person negative present of دانستن 'I do not know' (نـ + دانم). به گیتی is the locative 'in the world'. چو is the comparative 'like/as', linking to the proper name اسفندیار 'Esfandiyār' as the standard of comparison.
segments.s415-g32: برای here functions adverbially 'in respect of / as to' (i.e. 'in prowess'). و links two qualities; به مردی یکی نامدار is a verbless nominal clause 'among men, one famed' — به (to/among), مردی 'a man', یکی 'one/unique', نامدار 'renowned'. Together: 'in prowess and among men he is a uniquely renowned one.'

sentences.s415-s21: When Bahman came into the curtain-hall, he stood before his father at his feet.
sentences.s415-s22: Farrokh Esfandiyar asked him what answer that famed hero had given.
sentences.s415-s23: When he heard, he sat down before his father and told what he had heard — the ill tidings about Badr.
sentences.s415-s24: First he offered his salutations to Rostam; afterwards he recalled/recounted his words.
sentences.s415-s29: A supplication came to the king's presence; I do not know what secret he has with you.
sentences.s415-s30: Esfandiyar was angered at (or because of) Bahman, and he made him despised before the assembly.
sentences.s415-s31: He said to him: that one of the honorable people is not fit to sit with a woman in private.
sentences.s415-s32: And if you raise (rear) children to be grown and send them forth, they will not be brave and great.
sentences.s415-s17: Evil deeds do not come from a man of wisdom; he has seen no evil deed in us.
sentences.s415-s18: Zavareh came near Zal, and at that sight Rostam raised his crest.
sentences.s415-s19: He came to the skirts as far as the banks of the Helmand; his head grew keen from fear of harm.
sentences.s415-s20: He tightened the reins and pressed forward toward the river; he continued until Bahman brought greetings.
sentences.s415-s5: Place a golden throne in the iwans; go and set the kingly ceremonial robe.
sentences.s415-s6: Just so — at the time of King Kavus, the foundation there was even more substantial.
sentences.s415-s7: Prepare whatever is required for the dishes — good stews, one after another, produced by careful preparation.
sentences.s415-s8: For (it is) that the king's son has come near us; he has come full of hatred and eager for battle.
sentences.s415-s25: He told his father everything he had seen, yet that very thing he kept unseen and concealed.
sentences.s415-s26: He said to him that, since no one had seen Rostam at Piltan for the gathering...
sentences.s415-s27: He has a lion's heart and the body of a ragged elephant; he can lift whales from the Nile.
sentences.s415-s28: He now came to the bank of the Helmand, with a blue cuirass, himself, a mace and a lasso.
sentences.s415-s1: When Bahman heard the speech from Rostam, he set out with the pure-bodied mobed.
sentences.s415-s2: Tahmatan remained on the road for a while; he summoned Farāmarz of Zowara before him.
sentences.s415-s3: From here, go close to Dastān; come to my presence in Kabulistan.
sentences.s415-s4: Tell them: Kaspandiyar has come; someone who desires the world has arrived.
sentences.s415-s9: He is said to be renowned and a brave king; he did not think that the battle would be like a plain of lions.
sentences.s415-s10: May I stand before him; if he accepts the promise, there will be hope of good for everyone.
sentences.s415-s11: If I perceive any goodness in his heart, I will bring for his helmet rubies and gold.
sentences.s415-s12: I do not begrudge him treasure and jewels — neither bracers nor a gorget nor sword.
sentences.s415-s33: Where have you seen the rebels who have heard a fox's cry?
sentences.s415-s34: for Rostam will indeed make a war-elephant and will shatter the famed heart of the assembly.
sentences.s415-s35: Thus he said privately to Peshotan that this lion, a warrior, is a maker of war.
sentences.s415-s36: He keeps making youth out of himself; from his years, truly, no breaker has come.
sentences.s415-s13: And if I bring him back, do not be hopeless for me — my day is bright with him.
sentences.s415-s14: You know that that well‑twisted lasso brings even an elephant's bare head into bonds.
sentences.s415-s15: Zavāra said to him: 'Do not suppose that anyone henceforth seeks battle; there is no one who fights for hatred/vengeance.'
sentences.s415-s16: I do not know in the world anyone like Esfandiyār; in prowess and as a man he is uniquely renowned.
